name: product-ux
description: BeSpunky product & UX design skills. keep-users-oriented: whenever you make someone wait or move them through a process, answer the three questions - what is the expected result, where am I in the process, what is the next step - and pick the right feedback for deterministic vs nondeterministic processes. A universal service-design principle, expressed primarily through software UI (loading/progress, async ops, multi-step flows, long-running jobs, notifications, optimistic UI). redesign-means-rethink: when asked to REDESIGN a UI (a layout, screen, page, component, or flow), treat it as a complete creative reconception FROM SCRATCH, never a modification or reskin of existing code - the existing implementation has ZERO design authority, do not read it to inform the new design or even look at it before conceiving the new one; reconceive the FORM from intent/requirements/feeling, honor the PURPOSE (what it is for, its data and functionality) which comes from the spec and the user not from reverse-engineering the old layout, and read the old code ONLY AFTER the new design exists to plan cleanup/overwrite/migration and confirm what functionality must survive, then build clean and remove the old. A targeted tweak (move a button, change a colour) is NOT a redesign. The anti-anchoring entry gate to the experience-design trio (envision -> stage-the-vision -> realize, run from scratch). envision-the-experience: imagine the world an interface lives in - its feeling, place, and metaphor - before any layout or component, and design every element as a native inhabitant of that world rather than a generic control on a page; the screen is a place, not a page. Interrogate every element the spec names (a 'menu' might become a sunflower whose petals you pick), speak in sensory/non-technical language naming no implementation, keep immersive from becoming busy (restraint over spectacle), and produce a Vision. stage-the-vision: the visual architect and creative MIDDLE of the trio - the Vision says what a screen should FEEL like but not what it should LOOK like, and grounding alone can't make ART (it only stops the generic/garish). This skill invents the bold, web-native artistic MOMENTS that turn the feeling into something you'd screenshot, staying at the ART level: it speaks the web's language (parallax, cinematic scroll, a character that turns to camera, a living background, type-as-image, reveal choreography) but describes what artful thing happens and how it feels, never how to build it (that's realize). The altitude ladder: envision speaks pure feeling (zero web words); stage-the-vision speaks web-native art ('the room is a parallax background that deepens on scroll; a lion's face fills the hero and turns to meet your eyes'); realize speaks engineering. Because a model isn't a native artist, it reaches art by inventing several BOLD concepts and choosing the striking-yet-true, STEALING from specific great work (award sites, real art) and adapting its moves, composing with craft (focal point, scale, negative space, the cinematic moment), and sourcing genuinely artful assets - all grounded so it's not generic, restrained so it's not garish (bold != loud), and judged by an OUTSIDE EYE for beauty (never self-certified). Produces the Staging (a bold concept + concrete described moments + a visual system), confirmed at low fidelity before high-fidelity build. Each moment is staged across form factors, mobile-first (re-staged for a phone, not shrunk). Routes to a reference library (the web as an art medium; inventing the concept; composition & cinematic staging incl. staging across form factors; stealing from great work; grounding, restraint & the taste gate). realize-the-vision: the craftsman that turns a Vision AND a confirmed Staging into a real, running interface by RESEARCHING the truest, soundest means before writing any code - reads the Vision as a contract (the feeling must survive), surveys the field (GSAP, Motion, three.js/R3F/angular-three, Web Animations, CSS scroll-driven animations, View Transitions, Lottie/Rive, Canvas/SVG/WebGL, Web Audio, haptics), weighs tradeoffs and caveats (jank, layout thrash, reduced-motion, accessibility, mobile), and knows when to build in code vs. when to SOURCE an asset - figurative/photoreal art (a couple kissing, a landscape) is generated, licensed, or downloaded, never hand-coded into path-soup, handling licensing, review, and art-direction. Chooses deliberately for the vision AND the constraints, designs and confirms the architecture before building (architecture-first), wraps imperative libraries behind clean Angular seams, and engineers each staged moment. Requires a confirmed Staging before high-fidelity build (else it invokes stage-the-vision first), never invents the art itself, never self-certifies aesthetics (an outside eye judges look), and verifies against the feeling and the Staging in the running app. Routes to a reference library (motion & timelines; depth/3D/spatial; scroll & cinematic; SVG/canvas/generative; build-vs-source & sourcing/generating assets; performance & budgets; responsive & adaptive layout - engineering each moment's per-form-factor staging, re-composing rather than shrinking; accessibility/reduced-motion/fallbacks; sound & haptics). The building member of the trio envision-the-experience -> stage-the-vision -> realize-the-vision (feeling -> Staging -> build).
